rjr: C3 was an usual response to the F4 approach
column2: normally one would want to extend up the left side or pincer from the lower right.

column1: rjr: one problem with the H16 move is that the territory it appears to be defending (the upper middle side) is open on the left (which means an opponent can jump in there somewhat and reduce it)
rjr: closing off that open side with E18 would seem best first if you want to try to make the upper middle serious territory (which it could be)
rjr: connecting with H12 seems premature - better to cut at J10 first and then push
rjr: N4 feels slow - if it's an attack move then perhaps N3
column2: if it's building territory then perhaps K7 or O10 or E18 is still available.  or a stronger attack would probably be dropping into the corner at R2.

column1: rjr: T14 is an end-game move
column2: best to save those for later unless you're planning a killing attack

column1: rjr: L17 feels like it's going in the wrong direction but i see how it strengthens the top middle group
rjr: but N18 gives W monkey-jumps in both directions
rjr: P14 doesn't do much as a cutting stone because the W groups are somewhat connected (especially after the solid closing at T13.
rjr: better to use it as a sacrifice stone perhaps
rjr: M13 is an attempt to cut most of the cuts around the upper-middle W group
rjr: P6 is too close to the thickness of the lower-right B wall - if nothing else it could be a clamp on the Q8 stone
rjr: definitely better as a cutting stone - i didn't realize there were so many ways that could go wrong for W.
rjr: N11 is connecting 2 very weak groups across the middle - unless they can break thru or make life they're captures
rjr: O12 sacrifices the useless stone at O14 in exchange for a more solid connection (O14 doesn't add as much value as a more solid wall across the middle
rjr: E3 is... an attack on the lower-left W group?
rjr: F1 is also an end-game move - to save F2 i would have played E2 as it also gives you a sente closing
rjr: D2 leaves you with gote and thus W can play away (or at least ahead)
rjr: N9 is also sacrificing a less useful (or more likely unsaveable) stone.
rjr: at A10 i could see the 2 white stones couldn't be saved and thus the territory they walled in was less valuable
rjr: well, i guess they could be saved but still the territory was greatly reduced.
rjr: a few ways to handle the monkey jump depending on surrounding stones and whether you want to risk the ko
rjr: i considered the snapback at M3 but it left the cut at J5 and i didn't like that cut but perhaps i could have risked it
rjr: O5 was unneccessary - you already had sufficient eyes
rjr: A15 should have come after the push at A17
rjr: either way it's gote to B
rjr: but playing A17 first guarantees an extra ponit
rjr: H14 was also premature -could push at K14 first
rjr: granted it doesn't cost W any points so likely there's a better move elsewhere
rjr: A17 is now a point in sente (the best kind)
rjr: there were about 6 1-point sente moves i think for B at this point
rjr: no need to play A9 until you've tried the ko
rjr: R5 might not have been neccessary
rjr: those 2 B groups are huge and somewhat connected - any W stone played in there is dead as soon as it touches the board
rjr: M18 is dame - there's still the ko on the board
rjr: after M14 K14 is a point in sente
rjr: i think R17 was unnecessary
rjr: i take that back - it could have led to a very weird seki
rjr: i wish i could have seen this sequence. :)  i'm not sure how to avoid the seki in the top-right after the cut at R16, unless it involves sacrificing some stones
